relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Temple teaching and good news draw the authorities near.
They challenge the source of his authority.
Jesus answers with a question.
John’s baptism becomes the test.
Their reasoning exposes calculation rather than truth.
Fear of the people traps their answer.
They choose not knowing.
Jesus refuses to satisfy their authority trap.
The vineyard is entrusted to farmers over time.
Fruit is sought, but the servant is beaten and sent empty.
Another servant is dishonored.
A third is wounded and cast out.
The beloved son is sent as the final appeal.
The heir exposes possessive violence.
The son is thrown outside the vineyard.
The vineyard is taken from fruitless tenants.
The rejected stone becomes the corner-head.
The stone becomes crisis for all who oppose it.
The leaders know the parable has found them.
Spies pretend righteousness to trap his word.
Flattery masks the trap.
Caesar’s tribute is posed as the snare.
Jesus perceives the craftiness.
Image and inscription reveal the coin’s allegiance.
Give Caesar what bears Caesar’s image and the Divine what bears divine claim.
His answer leaves them silent.
Sadducees question from denial of resurrection.
They cite Moses and levirate duty.
The seven-brother scenario begins.
The second enters the same pattern.
All seven leave no children.
The woman also dies.
They reduce resurrection to a legal puzzle.
This age has marriage patterns.
That age and resurrection life are not simply this age extended.
Resurrection children are alive beyond death’s claim.
Moses at the bush witnesses the living Divine of the ancestors.
All live to the Divine.
Some scribes acknowledge the beauty of his answer.
Questioning falls silent.
Jesus asks how Messiah is David’s son.
David speaks of Lord at the right hand.
Enemies become footstool.
Messiah exceeds a simple lineage category.
Jesus warns disciples while the people listen.
Status-seeking religious performance is dangerous.
Long prayers can hide exploitation of widows, and judgment is greater.
